3: Ireland is under great pressure to meet the national targets on renewable energy recovery and greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions mitigation. In 2018, renewable energy made up only 11% of the gross final consumption in Ireland, not on track to meet the 2020 target of 16% SEAI (2020b). Ireland ranked third in per capita GHG emissions in the EU in 2017 (CSO, 2019). The high usage of fossil fuels, e.g. oil and natural gas, resulted in a 20% higher carbon di- oxide (CO2) emissions intensity than the European average, and the
second lowest share of renewable heat in the EU in 2018 (SEAI, 2020a). All these indicate the urgency of increasing renewable energy generation and mitigating GHG emissions.

Organic wastes from the agricultural and food sectors, e.g. pig manure (PM) and food waste (FW), are rich in biodegradable matters and have great energy potential (O'Shea et al., 2016). The agriculture sector contributes to the largest GHG emissions of 33% in Ireland (CSO, 2019). Approximately 3.2 million tonnes of PM is produced annually and most of it is directly land applied after storage (Jiang et al., 2018c). However, the present PM management method is inefficient in bioenergy recovery and causes GHG emissions. Xie (2012) reported during the PM storage, the CH4 emission contributed to as high as 92.6%-95.6% of the total GHG emissions, which could be avoided by the recovery of biomethane through anaerobic digestion (AD). So AD of PM is promising for both renewable bioenergy recovery and GHG emissions mitigation

(Dennehy et al., 2017b). In 2017, about 246,000 tonnes of biode- gradable municipal waste (comprising mainly FW) was managed in Ireland, with 74% by composting and 26% by AD (Epa, 2019). A life cycle assessment (LCA) review on AD and composting of FW in- dicates that AD is superior to composting due to less GHG emis- sions and odor (Lin et al., 2018). Therefore alternative management strategies for PM and FW are in urgent need.

Mono-digestion of PM has been proven financially inviable due to its low biomethane yield and ammonia inhibition (Dennehy et al., 2017a), while FW mono-digestion is easily inhibited by the high accumulation of volatile fatty acids (Jiang et al., 2018a). On- farm PM and FW co-digestion becomes economically feasible owing to the enhanced biomethane yield and revenue from FW gate fees (Dennehy et al., 2017a). LCA has been conducted for co- digestion of PM with organic wastes, e.g. organic residues (Vaneeckhaute et al., 2018) and energy crops (Lijó et al., 2014), but all of them are conducted under a specific substrate mixing ratio. The effects of various PM and FW mixing ratios on environmental impacts of the co-digestion system have not been reported. Obvious synergistic effects on the biomethane yield were reported in FW and PM co-digestion (Dennehy et al., 2016), but the intro- duction of FW would increase energy consumptions and GHG emissions owing to the collection, transportation and pre- treatment of FW. Whether the increased biomethane yield can offset these negative impacts are unknown and requires a careful assessment.

LCA has been conducted to assess environmental impacts of different digestate post treatment methods, e.g. direct digestate spreading, drying and pelletizing, and composting (Vázquez-Rowe et al., 2015). However, the comparison of nutrient profiles and ni- trogen use efficiency of different organic fertilizers (including raw PM, FW compost, FW digestate, and FW and PM co-digestate) are rarely reported. The introduction of FW into PM mono-digestion changes biodegradability of organic matters (Dennehy et al., 2016); this would affect nitrogen losses and subsequently the availability of nitrogen to plants after land application (WRAP, 2016). An overall LCA is necessary to give insights for farmers to conduct effective and precise fertilization of organic fertilizers.

LCA on organic waste management is usually conducted on the comparison of specific treatment methods, e.g. AD, composting, landfilling, and incineration (Guven et al., 2019). While in a real practice, the management of organic waste is diversified and pre- sents as a combination of different methods. Studies are rarely conducted to assess the environmental impacts of such an inte- grated waste management system. In this study, the environmental impacts of FW and PM co-digestion system were compared with the existing management practices in Ireland, i.e. direct land

application of PM, and composting and AD of FW. The methodology established in this study would provide a reference for the assessment of an integrated biowastes management system. The research objectives of this study were: (1) assessment of the environmental sustainability of different PM and FW management strategies; (2) analysis of the nutrient profiles and availabilities of different organic fertilizers; (3) identification of the hotspots for environmental impacts; and (4) determination of proper FW and PM mixing ratio for co-digestion systems.

4: 2.1. Life cycle assessment modeling and methodology

The LCA framework and principles are following ISO-14040 standard (ISO14040, 2006). Life cycle inventory (LCI) data is ob- tained from SimaPro software with Ecoinvent 3.5 database (Ecoinvent, 2018). The life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) is con- ducted using the CML-IA characterization method (CML, 2016). Eleven environmental impact categories can be assessed in this methodology, including fossil fuel depletion potential (FDP), global warming potential (GWP) over 100 years, eutrophication potential (EP), human toxicity potential (HTP), acidification potential (AP), photochemical oxidation potential, abiotic depletion potential (el- ements), ozone layer depletion potential, terrestrial ecotoxicity potential, freshwater aquatic ecotoxicity potential, and marine aquatic ecotoxicity potential. The first five categories are commonly used to assess on-farm management of PM (Vaneeckhaute et al., 2018; Whiting and Azapagic, 2014), which are discussed in detail in this study.
5: There are approximately 200 pig farms in Ireland with an average herd size of 762 SOWS (Teagasc, 2019). It leads to a total PM production of 16,000 3/yr at a production rate of 21 3//sow-yr) (Nolan et al., 2012). Presently, six AD facilities accept 64,000 t FW per year, with an average capacity of approximately 10,000 t/yr (EPA, 2019c). As a result, the function unit in this study is set as the PM production of 16,000 t/yr produced from an average size pig farm and the available FW amount of 10,000 t/yr. Using this unit rather than 1 tonne PM or FW is to provide a direct reference for farmers to estimate the environmental performance of the on-farm AD systems according to the farm sizes. 6: Two scenarios are set in this study. The Baseline scenario refers to existing PM and FW management practices in Ireland, in which PM is directly land applied, and FW is treated by composting (74% so 7400 t/yr) and AD (26% so 2,600 t/yr). The rejected FW during pre-treatment (5% of input FW) and residues in composting (5% of input FW) are sent for landfilling (McDougall et al., 2001). PM and FW co-digestion is compared as an alternative scenario. The con- struction and decommission of treatment infrastructures are excluded in this study, because these only occur once in the whole life span of treatment and their impacts are low compared to the operation stage (Esteves et al., 2019). One year's operation is assessed for each system.

Fig. 1 describes the system boundaries. In the Baseline, PM pro- duced on farm is applied to land after storage. The average storage time is 26 weeks to avoid application in unsuitable weather and ground conditions, or during spreading prohibited period (DAFM, 2014). FW is weekly collected from a transfer station and trans- ported to the composting and AD plants by lorries. Composting is processed according to the commercial practice in Ireland (Thorntons, 2020). The FW is inspected, shredded, and then sent for preliminary composting for 2-3 weeks. The product is screened as find compost (<12 mm), coarse compost (12-40 mm) and residues. The fine compost is further matured for 4-5 weeks, then pasteurized and stored for land application. The coarse compost is sent back to the preliminary composting system as the seed. The residues and rejected materials are sent to landfill. The landfill gas is recovered for electricity generation, flared or directly emitted.

During FW mono-digestion, and FW and PM co-digestion, a 7- day storage tank is needed to ensure the continuous feeding of FW. FW is shredded, screened, macerated, and then sent for pasteurization. In Ireland, pasteurization is not applied to PM from the same farm where the AD plant is located. But to avoid cross contamination, FW imported from outside the farm needs to be shredded to <12 mm particle size and pasteurized at 70 °C for 1 h (McDonnell et al., 2018). Then the pasteurized FW is sent for AD or mixed with PM for co-digestion. Combined heat and power (CHP) technology is used to recover electricity and heat from the biogas. In both co-digestion systems, the heat is used to supply energy for maintaining proper temperatures of FW pasteurization, AD, and pig houses. The excess heat is assumed to be utilized by surrounding residents, and the heat loss is not counted. The elec- tricity generated is exported to the grid network. The digestate is stored for 26 weeks and sent for land application.

FW is weekly collected from a transfer station and trans- ported to the composting and AD plants by lorries. Composting is processed according to the commercial practice in Ireland (Thorntons, 2020). The FW is inspected, shredded, and then sent for preliminary composting for 2-3 weeks. The product is screened as find compost (<12 mm), coarse compost (12-40 mm) and residues. The fine compost is further matured for 4-5 weeks, then pasteurized and stored for land application. The coarse compost is sent back to the preliminary composting system as the seed. The residues and rejected materials are sent to landfill. The landfill gas is recovered for electricity generation, flared or directly emitted.

During FW mono-digestion, and FW and PM co-digestion, a 7- day storage tank is needed to ensure the continuous feeding of FW. FW is shredded, screened, macerated, and then sent for pasteurization. In Ireland, pasteurization is not applied to PM from the same farm where the AD plant is located. But to avoid cross contamination, FW imported from outside the farm needs to be shredded to <12 mm particle size and pasteurized at 70 °C for 1 h (McDonnell et al., 2018). Then the pasteurized FW is sent for AD or mixed with PM for co-digestion. Combined heat and power (CHP) technology is used to recover electricity and heat from the biogas. In both co-digestion systems, the heat is used to supply energy for maintaining proper temperatures of FW pasteurization, AD, and pig houses. The excess heat is assumed to be utilized by surrounding residents, and the heat loss is not counted. The elec- tricity generated is exported to the grid network. The digestate is stored for 26 weeks and sent for land application.

Indirect N20 emissions can be caused by deposition of NH3 in volatilization and denitrifi- cation of NO3 in leaching/runoff, with the conversion factors of 0.01 kg N20-N/kg NH3-N and 0.0075 kg N20-N/kg NO-N, respectively (IPCC, 2019b).
12: <header>2.4.2. Food waste composting and residues landfilling </header>

The FW is collected from transfer stations, but the distances to composting and AD plants in Ireland are absent. Taking the UK as a reference, the distances from the collection areas to composting and AD plants range 4.2-26.7 km (CHU, 2015), with an average value of 9.7 km. Therefore a default value of 10 km is used in this study. During FW composting, the proportions of rejected FW, fine compost, coarse compost and residues are 5%, 36%, 20% and 5% of the total input FW, respectively, with the remaining 34% lost by respiration and evaporation (McDougall et al., 2001). The rejected FW and composting residues are sent to landfilling. The LCI for FW collection, transportation, and residual material landfilling are collected from Ecoinvent database 3.5 (Ecoinvent, 2018). The total solids (TS) content, and the N, P and K contents of the fine compost are referred to Favoino and Hogg (2008). The landfill gas produced from compost residues is 100 m³/t waste with the CH4 content of 52.8% (McDougall et al., 2001). In Ireland, about 43.3% of the landfill gas is used for electricity generation by engine with the efficiency of 28%, 16.4% is flared, and 40.3% is directly emitted to the air (EPA, 2019b). The electricity, diesel and water consumptions in com- posting are based on Martínez-Blanco et al. (2009), and the elec- tricity and diesel inputs for landfilling are collected from Turner et al. (2016). The LCI for electricity, heat, diesel and water pro- ductions are gathered from Ecoinvent database 3.5 (Ecoinvent, 2018). The air emissions and NO3 leaching/runoff during com- posting and land application processes are presented in Table A.3. The typical engine and flare emissions during landfill gas utilization in Ireland are collected from EPA (2012) (Table A.4).
13: The AD in Ireland is usually conducted in two series tanks at mesophilic conditions. The hydraulic retention time (HRT) for each tank is 25 days with a total HRT of 50 days (Dennehy et al., 2017a). The emissions of different processes in FW mono-digestion and PM/FW co-digestion systems are detailed in Table A.5 and A.6, respectively. The specific methane yields (SMYs) used in this study are 439 and 400 mL CH4/g VSadded for FW mono-digestion and PM/ FW co-digestion systems, respectively (detailed in Section 4.3). The packaged CHP system with a gas fueled reciprocating engine is used in this study. The electricity and heat efficiencies of CHP are 40% and 45%, respectively (Dennehy et al., 2017a).

The electricity demand during AD varies greatly in the literature, ranging from 5.4 kWh/t substrate (Dennehy et al., 2017a) to 65 kWh/t substrate (Salemdeeb et al., 2018). The specific electricity demands for mono- and co-digestion systems in this study are

detailed in Table A.7 and A.8. The total electricity demands for mono- and co-digestion are 36.4 kWh/t and 19.4 kWh/t substrate, respectively, according to different processes and scales of AD plants.

The heat demands during AD and pasteurization are calculated according to Nolan et al. (2012). The temperatures for AD and pasteurization are 35 °C and 70 °C, respectively. The average ambient temperature is 10 °C in Ireland (CSO, 2019). The TS con- tents in the digesters are calculated to be 5.0% and 6.6% in mono- and co-digestion systems, respectively, based on the volatile solids (VS) removals (detailed in Section 4.3). So the specific heat of water (4180 J/kg/°C) is used to represent the heat values of the digestates to calculate the heat demand of AD. The specific heat of FW is calculated based on the compositions of carbohydrate, protein, lipid, fiber, ash and water (Table 1) according to ASHRAE (2018). As a result, the overall specific heat value of FW is 3580 J/kg/°C. The volume of each digester in series is calculated based on the HTR of 25 days, and the volume of pasteurization tank is based on the daily FW amount accepted. The heat transfer coefficients for the concrete wall above the ground (insulated), concrete floor in contact with dry earth, and floating cover (insulated) are 0.8, 1.8 and 1.0 W/ m²/°C, respectively (Zhang, 2013). The heat contained in pasteur- ized FW is considered to compensate the heat losses in digesters. The heat demand of pig houses is 728 kWh/sow/yr (Dennehy et al., 2017a). The overall mass and energy balances in mono- and co- digestion systems are detailed in Table 2.

There are various uncertainties in the modeling parameters like electricity demand, transportation distances, and different emis- sion factors. So for the co-digestion system, sensitivity analysis is conducted by altering the default value of one parameter to its minimum or maximum value each time. The variation ranges of each parameter are described in previous sections and Table A.6. The tested parameters include electricity demand, transportation distance, and high emission factors, including CH4 emissions during AD, CHP, and digestate storage; NH3 emissions during digestate storage and land application; and direct N20 emissions and NO3 leaching/runoff during land application.

There are various uncertainties in the modeling parameters like electricity demand, transportation distances, and different emis- sion factors. So for the co-digestion system, sensitivity analysis is conducted by altering the default value of one parameter to its minimum or maximum value each time. The variation ranges of each parameter are described in previous sections and Table A.6. The tested parameters include electricity demand, transportation distance, and high emission factors, including CH4 emissions during AD, CHP, and digestate storage; NH3 emissions during digestate storage and land application; and direct N20 emissions and NO3 leaching/runoff during land application.
18: The AD in Ireland is usually conducted in two series tanks at mesophilic conditions. The hydraulic retention time (HRT) for each tank is 25 days with a total HRT of 50 days (Dennehy et al., 2017a). The emissions of different processes in FW mono-digestion and PM/FW co-digestion systems are detailed in Table A.5 and A.6, respectively. The specific methane yields (SMYs) used in this study are 439 and 400 mL CH4/g VSadded for FW mono-digestion and PM/ FW co-digestion systems, respectively (detailed in Section 4.3). The packaged CHP system with a gas fueled reciprocating engine is used in this study. The electricity and heat efficiencies of CHP are 40% and 45%, respectively (Dennehy et al., 2017a).

The electricity demand during AD varies greatly in the literature, ranging from 5.4 kWh/t substrate (Dennehy et al., 2017a) to 65 kWh/t substrate (Salemdeeb et al., 2018). The specific electricity demands for mono- and co-digestion systems in this study are

detailed in Table A.7 and A.8. The total electricity demands for mono- and co-digestion are 36.4 kWh/t and 19.4 kWh/t substrate, respectively, according to different processes and scales of AD plants.

The heat demands during AD and pasteurization are calculated according to Nolan et al. (2012). The temperatures for AD and pasteurization are 35 °C and 70 °C, respectively. The average ambient temperature is 10 °C in Ireland (CSO, 2019). The TS con- tents in the digesters are calculated to be 5.0% and 6.6% in mono- and co-digestion systems, respectively, based on the volatile solids (VS) removals (detailed in Section 4.3). So the specific heat of water (4180 J/kg/°C) is used to represent the heat values of the digestates to calculate the heat demand of AD. The specific heat of FW is calculated based on the compositions of carbohydrate, protein, lipid, fiber, ash and water (Table 1) according to ASHRAE (2018). As a result, the overall specific heat value of FW is 3580 J/kg/°C. The volume of each digester in series is calculated based on the HTR of 25 days, and the volume of pasteurization tank is based on the daily FW amount accepted. The heat transfer coefficients for the concrete wall above the ground (insulated), concrete floor in contact with dry earth, and floating cover (insulated) are 0.8, 1.8 and 1.0 W/ m²/°C, respectively (Zhang, 2013). The heat contained in pasteur- ized FW is considered to compensate the heat losses in digesters. The heat demand of pig houses is 728 kWh/sow/yr (Dennehy et al., 2017a). The overall mass and energy balances in mono- and co- digestion systems are detailed in Table 2.

19: 3.1. Energy recovery, direct GHG emissions and nutrient availability

The energy recovery, direct GHG emissions and nutrient avail- abilities of the Baseline and co-digestion scenarios are shown in Fig. 2. In the Baseline, the energy recovery mainly comes from mono-digestion of FW in forms of CHP produced electricity and heat (2335 MWh/yr), with only 47 MWh/yr electricity being from landfill gas. In the co-digestion scenario, 10,046 MWh/yr is recov- ered by CHP, which is 4.2 time as much as the Baseline. The embodied energy in PM and FW can be fully recovered for utilization.

Direct GHG emissions of different management systems are analyzed separately to identify the high emission processes, and identify specific measures for GHG emissions mitigation. In the Baseline scenario, the direct GHG emissions during PM land application are 398 CO2eq/yr, with PM storage and land application contributing to 69% and 31%, respectively. CH4 and N20 account for 51% and 49% of the direct emissions, respectively. The direct GHGs during FW composting are 570 t CO2eq/yr. Half of the emissions comes from the directly emitted landfill gas, and the composting process accounts for 32%. CH4 and N20 account for 62% and 38% of the direct emissions. The direct GHGs during FW mono-digestion are 643 t CO2eq/yr, of which CH4 and N20 emissions account for 84% and 16%, respectively. Digestate storage contributes to the highest proportion of 51%, followed by AD (33%, including CHP). The overall direct GHGs in the Baseline scenario are 1610 CO2eq/yr, with CH4 and N20 emissions accounting for 68% (1094 t CO2eq/yr) and 32% (512 t CO2eq/yr), respectively.

In the co-digestion scenario, the total direct GHGs are 2745 t CO2eq/yr, 70% higher than that in the Baseline. But the high biogas credit can compensate the increased GHG emissions, which will be detailed in Section 3.2. The highest emission comes from digestate storage (52%), followed by AD (29%, including CHP). The CH4 emission accounts for 81% (2233 t CO2eq/yr) of the total direct GHGs, with N20 only contributing to 19% (512 t CO2eq/yr). Obvi- ously, the increased GHGs mainly come from CH4 emission. Compared with PM land application and FW composting, co- digestion of them accelerates the degradation of organic carbon to CH4, but also leads to higher emissions during digestate storage (1409 t CO2eq/yr) and fugitive losses in AD (768 t CO2eq/yr). Therefore reducing CH4 emission during digestate storage and fugitive CH4 emissions during AD are practical methods to mitigate direct GHG emissions.
In the Baseline scenario, the availabilities of N in PM land application, FW composting and AD are 45%, 32% and 44%,

respectively, with an overall value of 34%. The available N in co- digestion scenario is 43%, higher than the overall value in the Baseline. The land areas required for raw PM, FW compost and digestate are 240, 142 and 127 ha, respectively (totally 509 ha). The co-digestion digestate needs a higher land area of 676 ha, which is the result of higher N availability in the digestate. During PM land application, N is mainly lost in land application, accounting for 45% of the total input N. In FW composting, only N retained in the fine compost can be recovered. The low fine compost productivity (36%) determines the low N availability, even though the total N losses during land application of compost are much lower than PM and digestate. The N losses in mono- and co-digestion systems mainly happen in digestate land application, accounting for 52%-54% of the total N. As a result, reducing N losses in land application is critical to increase N utilization efficiency.

23: The overall environmental impacts of the two scenarios are shown in Table 3. Positive and negative values represent environ- mental impacts and environmental benefits, respectively. As can be seen, the co-digestion scenario performs better than the Baseline in 9 of the 11 environmental categories, except for AP and EP. Compared with the Baseline, the freshwater aquatic, marine aquatic and terrestrial ecotoxicities in the co-digestion scenario are decreased by 5-30 times; and the ozone layer depletion, photo- chemical oxidation and abiotic resources depletion are decreased by 10, 5 and 1 times, respectively. The overall environmental im- pacts and detailed contributions of different processes to GWP, FDP, AP, EP and HTP are shown in Fig. 3.
24: The FDP refers to the consumption of non-renewable fossil fuels. Both scenarios show net benefits due to the biogas and fertilizer credits. Compared with the Baseline, the net FDP (without credits) in the co-digestion scenario is increased by 40%; but when the biogas and fertilizer credits are counted, the overall FDP in the co- digestion is decreased by 506%. It means the recovered energy can not only offset the increased energy consumption caused by the introduction of FW, but also provide extra energy for benefits. In the Baseline, composting causes 36% of the net impacts, follows by PM processing (24%) and FW AD (19%). In the co-digestion scenario, 85% of the net FDP is attributed to energy consumptions during AD, with heat and electricity demands contributing to 60% and 26%, respectively. Therefore the key point to decrease FDP in co- digestion scenario is to control the electricity and heat consump- tions in AD.
25: The GWP qualifies the contribution of air emissions to climate change over 100 years. Compared with the Baseline, the net GWP (without credits) increased by 69% in the co-digestion scenario, but the total GWP is decreased by 213% due to the high biogas credit. The GHG equivalent of biogas credit is 4096 t CO2eq/yr in the co- digestion scenario, indicating co-digestion can greatly mitigate GHG emissions. In the Baseline, FW composting (30%) and PM processing (20%) cause half of the GWP. In the co-digestion sce- nario, AD (46%) and digestate storage (40%) are main processes causing GWP. In both scenarios, direct GHGs are main contributors to GWP, accounting for 75-76% of the net impacts. So the key points for GWP mitigation are reducing direct GHGs, as described before.

31: The effects of key emission factors on different environmental categories in co-digestion are shown in Fig. 4. The CH4 emission mainly affects GWP. The digestate storage process has the highest effect; decreasing the emission factor to the minimum of 2.20% reduces the overall GWP by 105% and increasing it to the maximum value of 11.22% increases the GWP by 117%. The CH4 emission during AD is determined by the air tightness of digesters and pipelines. The minimum and maximum fugitive CH4 emissions during AD decreases and increases the GWP by both 37%. The CH4 emission in CHP may occur through pipes, engine, and incomplete combustion. A minimum emission factor of 1.03% reduces the GWP by 21%, while a maximum value of 2.34% increases it by 11%.

The NH3 emission has great impacts on GWP, AP and EP. During digestate land application, NH3 emission varies greatly and causes significant uncertainty. Reducing the emission factor to the mini- mum of 21% decreases the overall GWP, AP and EP by 25%, 47%, and 33%, respectively; and increasing it to the maximum of 65% in- creases the three categories by 33%, 61% and 43%, respectively. The effects of NH3 emission during digestate storage are relatively lower. The minimum emission factor of 0.034 g NH3-N/kWhe1 re- sults in 2%, 9% and 5% decreases in GWP, AP and EP; while the maximum value of 3.68 g NH3-N/kWhe increases these categories by 3%, 13% and 7%, respectively. N20 emissions during land appli- cation mainly affect GWP, with the minimum (0.09%) and maximum (0.88%) emission factors causing 20% decrease and 24%

increase, respectively. The NO3 leaching from digestate land application affects GWP and EP greatly. A minimum NO3 leaching of 6.2% reduces GWP and EP by 10% and 15%, and a maximum value of 38% increases the two categories by 28% and 42%, respectively.

33: Electricity demand during the co-digestion scenario has great influences on GWP, FDP and HTP. Compared with the default model (19.4 kWh/t substrate), a minimum electricity demand of 5.4 kWh/t substrate decreases the GWP, FDP and HTP by 24%, 6% and 7%, respectively; while the maximum value of 65 kWh/t substrate in- creases the three categories by 76%, 18% and 21%, respectively. The transportation distance mainly impacts GWP, FDP and HTP. A minimum distance of 4.2 km decreases the GWP, FDP and HTP by 3%, 2% and 3%, respectively, while the maximum distance of 26.7 km increases the GWP, FDP and HTP by 10%, 5% and 39%, respectively. These results can provide reference for farmers to estimate the environmental impacts of their AD plants according to the practical electricity consumptions and the distances of their farms to FW collection and digestate application areas.

38: In the co-digestion of FW and PM, the hotspots greatly impacting environmental impacts are CH4 emissions during digestate storage and AD, N losses during digestate land applica- tion, and energy consumptions for AD operation.

CH4 emissions during digestate storage and AD (including CHP) make up 82% of the direct GHG emissions. Whiting and Azapagic (2014) reported that 86% of the GWP is due to digestate storage. Hou et al. (2015) reported acidification of slurry to the pH of 4.5-6.5 during storage can significantly decrease CH4 emission by 87%, and the emission can be suppressed by 50%-100% with various storage covers. Besides, maintaining good airtightness of digesters and CHP equipment, and increasing the combustion ef- ficiency of CHP can also reduce fugitive CH4 emissions and emis- sions from incomplete combustion.

N losses during digestate land application are critical to AP and EP, and determine the N availability of the digestate. NH3 emissions contribute to 85% and 89% of the AP and EP, respectively. It agrees with Whiting and Azapagic (2014) that 95% and 97% of the AP and

Fig. 5. Changes of specific methane yield and volatile solids removal with food waste proportions in the substrate mixture based on VS (the scatters are experiment data, and the dash lines are simulation results with the modified Weibull model).
Fig. 6. Effects of food waste proportions in the substrate mixture on various environmental impacts (the black scatters indicate the default model).

EP are caused by NH3 emissions, respectively. NH3 emissions and NO3 leaching during land application account for 89% of the total N losses. NH3 emissions can be controlled by effective land applica- tion methods. Compared with the surface broadcasting, band spreading, injection, and incorporation of PM can decrease NH3 emissions by 55%, 80%, and 70%, respectively (Hou et al., 2015). While NO3 leaching is mainly affected by season, the NO3 leaching factors of 30%-38% in autumn are much higher than those in spring (6.2%-18%) due to high precipitation and low plant uptake (Weslien et al., 1998). WRAP (2016) also reported total N losses in

autumn were as high as 55%-75%, while only 20%-30% were lost in spring. A good application method at the proper season is critical for farmers to reduce environmental impacts and increase N use efficiency.

The energy consumption in AD operation also determines the environmental sustainability of the co-digestion system, and con- tributes to 85%, 77% and 46% of the FFD, HTP and GWP, respectively. Dry co-digestion may provide a promising solution for reducing energy consumption. Compared with wet AD with 5% TS content, dry AD at the TS content of 20% can reduce the reactor volume by 75%

without affecting the biomethane yield (Jiang et al., 2018c). The much smaller reactor volume requires less energy for heating, and less diesel consumptions for digestate transportation and land application. The electricity input is dominant in energy consump- tion. The electricity demand of the co-digestion system is usually higher than mono-digestion because of the pre-treatment of CO- substrates. Dennehy et al. (2017a) reported an electricity demand of 0 kWh/m ³ substrate for FW and PM co-digestion compared with 5.4 kWh/m³ for mono-digestion of PM. The AD plant scale affects electricity consumptions as well. Berglund and Börjesson (2006) reported the energy demands of farm-scale (10,000 t/yr) and large scale (20,000-60,000 t/yr) AD plants were 9.2 and 18.3 kWh/t substrates, respectively. Operation processes corresponding to biogas and digestate utilization also affect the electricity demand. For example, biogas upgrading for biomethane grid injection con- sumes 0.25 kWh/m³ biogas (Browne et al., 2011) and the electricity demand for solid/liquid separation of digestate is estimated at 0.8 kWh/t substrate (Nolan et al., 2012). Farmers and the gas industry can estimate the energy demand according to these factors.

Operation processes corresponding to biogas and digestate utilization also affect the electricity demand. For example, biogas upgrading for biomethane grid injection con- sumes 0.25 kWh/m³ biogas (Browne et al., 2011) and the electricity demand for solid/liquid separation of digestate is estimated at 0.8 kWh/t substrate (Nolan et al., 2012). Farmers and the gas industry can estimate the energy demand according to these factors.

CH4 emissions during digestate storage and AD (including CHP) make up 82% of the direct GHG emissions. Whiting and Azapagic (2014) reported that 86% of the GWP is due to digestate storage. Hou et al. (2015) reported acidification of slurry to the pH of 4.5-6.5 during storage can significantly decrease CH4 emission by 87%, and the emission can be suppressed by 50%-100% with various storage covers. Besides, maintaining good airtightness of digesters and CHP equipment, and increasing the combustion ef- ficiency of CHP can also reduce fugitive CH4 emissions and emis- sions from incomplete combustion.

43: <header>4.3. Effects of FW proportions in co-digestion scenario </header>

In the default model, 10,000 t/yr FW is directly collected from the transfer station representing the average FW amount accepted by AD facilities in Ireland. However, it is not practical for all pig farms to get such a high amount of FW. If the presently managed FW in Ireland (246,000 t/yr) is uniformly distributed to 200 pig farms, the average amount of FW for each farm is 1230 t/yr. Thus the FW amounts ranging from 1000 to 10,000 t/yr are discussed. The FW proportion of in the substrate mixture directly affects the SMY and VS removal of co-digestion. A modified empirical Weibull model proposed by Jiang et al. (2018b) is used to predict SMYs and VS removals under different FW proportions, based on experi- mental data reported in literature (Dennehy et al., 2016). As shown in Fig. 5, both curves are well simulated with R2 ranging 0.980-0.982. A 15% safety factor is applied to the simulated SMYs because the biomethane yield in full scale AD plants are typically lower than the values obtained in laboratory- and pilot-scale ex- periments (Nolan et al., 2012).

Fig. 6 shows the effects of FW proportions on various environ- mental impacts. The increase of FW proportion brings great bene- fits to FDP, GWP and HTP in the co-digestion scenario due to the increased biogas credit; but the impacts on AP and EP are increased due to more NH3 emission from the digestate. Therefore, proper NH3 emission mitigation methods should be applied. For every 1000 t/yr increase in FW (relative to 16,000 t PM/yr), the FDP, GWP and HTP in the co-digestion scenario can be decreased by 11% (3.8 TJ/yr), 29% (109 t CO2eq/yr) and 12% (25 t 1,4-DBeq/yr), respectively; and the AP and EP are increased by 6.8% (6.4 t SO2eq/ yr) and 7% (2.0 :PO4eq/yr), respectively. The GWP is positive at a low FW proportion, because the low biomethane production cannot offset the negative impacts of FW introduction. It becomes negative when the FW proportion increases to above 2000 t/yr (relative to 16,000 t PM/yr).

The results obtained in this study can provide references to farmers, the gas industry and policy makers. The analysis on N losses and mitigation methods help farmers to understand the N availability and perform effective fertilization. The effects of FW proportion, electricity consumption and transportation distance enable farmers and the gas industry to assess the environmental feasibility of the co-digestion system, and help to determine the selection of AD plant location. The environmental impacts and hotspot analysis are also beneficial for policy makers to make an environmentally sustainable decision for biowastes management strategy and to maximize environmental benefits.

46: This study compares the environmental impacts of present PM and FW management strategies with an alternative co-digestion scenario. The co-digestion scenario performs better in most envi- ronmental impacts, and the N availability of the digestate is supe- rior as well. Hotspots greatly influencing the environmental impacts are identified and mitigation methods are proposed. A low FW proportion in the co-digestion scenario cannot offset the negative impacts caused by the introduction of FW. For a farm with a PM production of 16,000 t/yr, at least 2000 t/yr FW is needed to ensure a negative GWP. These results can provide practical refer- ences to pig farmers, the gas industry and policy makers.
